problem_summary: 2021 MCM Problem C (data): Build a model to monitor and control the Asian Giant Hornet (Vespa mandarinia) invasion in Washington State. Using a provided dataset of public sighting reports (with lab status, comments, GlobalID-linked photos, lat/long, dates), the team had to: (1) predict how/where the hornet spreads, (2) screen/prioritize public sighting reports to use scarce expert resources efficiently, (3) define a rule for updating the model as new data arrives, and (4) determine when eradication can be declared. The paper builds an integrated pipeline tying nest-location inference, spread prediction, image classification, a priority-queue triage model, and an extinction/eradication statistic.
models_used: Poisson Distribution for nest location: workers' distance from nest modeled as Poisson; back-trace from confirmed sightings to a probability heatmap of nest location P_pt(long,lat) within a 10km radius of each sighting

Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP): 3x3 judging matrix over Distance/Tree/Elevation factors, eigenvalue method giving weights W=(0.285 distance, 0.498 tree, 0.217 elevation), consistency check CR=0.016 (<0.10) computed in Matlab

Elevation as a geographic proxy: min-max standardized ASL data scraped via Python web crawler from freemaptools elevation-finder; used elevation as a stand-in for tree coverage after observing they coincide on satellite maps
Hornet Spread Model: same Poisson + AHP machinery re-applied with ~30km annual dispersal to predict next-year nest locations
Convolutional Neural Network (ResNet-18/34/50, pretrained, fine-tuned to 13 classes in PyTorch): insect image classifier, SGD lr=0.005 weight_decay=1e-4 momentum=0.9, CrossEntropyLoss, 30 epochs
Natural Language Processing: noun extraction from 'Lab Comments' to identify the 13 insect species and auto-label photos by GlobalID
Web crawler data augmentation: scraped 200 images/species (13 species) from bugguide.net to enlarge the sparse training set

Prioritizing (triage) Model: distance-banded weighted-credibility scoring — Group 3 (>70km): c=p_vm only; Group 2 (20-70km): 0.6*p_vm + 0.4*p_spread; Group 1 (<20km): 0.5*p_vm + 0.2*p_spread + 0.3*p_nest; with a c<0.01 cull and resource allocation across groups

Solow-Roberts nonparametric sighting-record extinction test: p = (t_n - t_{n-1})/(T - t_{n-1}), extended to use average sighting frequency f and a second-order-difference frequency-change term c; eradication declared when p < 0.05
key_techniques: Inverse/back-tracing inference: treating observed worker sightings as samples of a Poisson distance distribution and inverting to a spatial likelihood field for the hidden nest

Domain-knowledge-driven feature engineering grounded in a real entomology reference (Matsura & Sakagami 1973): 2km mean / 8km max forage range, ~30km dispersal, tree-root nesting, May-Nov activity all pulled from literature and baked into parameters
Proxy substitution under data scarcity: using elevation as a surrogate for unavailable forest-cover data, explicitly justified via satellite imagery

Reframing an intractable task into a tractable one: instead of binary 'is this the hornet?' (too few positive examples), train 13-class species classifier, then collapse to true/false — boosting accuracy to ~98-99%
Transfer learning + ablation across model depths (ResNet-18/34/50) and across data conditions (website / uploaded / cropped) reported in honest comparison tables
Diagnosing and fixing a domain shift: recognizing public photos are blurry/off-center vs. website close-ups, then manually cropping to center the insect to lift uploaded-photo accuracy
Multi-model fusion via weighted credibility with distance-gated logic (the three groups), letting the photo CNN, spread model, and nest model each contribute by spatial context
Quantitative validation by back-testing the triage on the real May-Aug data: the 5 true hornet photos moved from ranks [84,138,166,190,1257] of 1400 to [1,2,3,4,16]
Confusion-matrix error analysis identifying specific failure modes (hornet confused with fly/hornet/bee; cicada killer mistaken for hornet)
why_it_won: "It directly and completely answers all four sub-questions of a notoriously open-ended data problem with an end-to-end, operationally usable pipeline rather than disconnected analyses. The standout move is the triage back-test: showing the five real hornet photos jump from buried ranks (84-1257 out of 1400) to the top of the queue (1-4 plus 16) is concrete, verifiable, decision-relevant evidence that the model actually saves overloaded experts time — exactly the WSDA's real pain point. Judges reward this kind of 'does it work on the actual data' proof. The team also showed strong modeling judgment: grounding every parameter in a real entomology paper, handling data scarcity with NLP auto-labeling + web-scraped augmentation, and cleverly reframing the under-sampled binary problem as a 13-class problem to reach ~98% usable accuracy. It is honest (reports the ugly 18-25% multiclass uploaded-photo accuracy alongside the good numbers), it builds a genuinely integrated system (each model feeds the next), it satisfies the self-update requirement with a clear yearly data-refresh rule, and it nails the Solow-Roberts eradication criterion with a defensible p<0.05 threshold. Strong communication — clean memo, a mocked user-interface figure, and clarity that a non-technical reader can follow — seals the Outstanding."
section_highlights: "Summary: front-loads the whole pipeline (Poisson nest-locating -> AHP+elevation spread -> NLP+crawler+CNN screening -> Solow-Roberts eradication) and states the headline results (98% photo accuracy, p<0.05 eradication rule). Assumptions section explicitly justifies each assumption AND ties it to the method that needs it (e.g., constant public attentiveness is named as the precondition for valid Solow-Roberts use) — a sophisticated touch. Section 5.3 (Improving Accuracy) is a model of honest iterative debugging: identifies the blurry/off-center domain shift, applies cropping, reports the modest 18.3%->25.23% gain, and doesn't oversell. Section 6.3 (Testing the Effect) is the persuasive core — the ranks [84,138,166,190,1257] -> [1,2,3,4,16] back-test. The Memorandum is plain-language, structured as a 3-step recipe, addressed to the WSDA, and references the included UI mockup (Fig 13) — exactly the requested deliverable. Appendix ships real runnable PyTorch + geopy queueing code, signaling the work is genuine."
pitfalls_or_limitations: Mathematical sloppiness in the Poisson usage: P(x)=λ^x/x! e^{-λ} is a discrete PMF but x is a continuous distance in km, and they plug raw geodesic km into x! (factorial of a distance) in both the formula and the code (math.factorial(int(dis))) — dimensionally and statistically incoherent, even though the qualitative 'closer = more likely' shape happens to be reasonable
Using elevation as a proxy for tree coverage is a convenient but weak substitution; the team itself flags forest-coverage and pine-tree preference as unaddressed in Weaknesses
Internal inconsistency/typos in the credibility formula: p_next vs p_nest naming, and a Group-2 weighting that arguably should use the new-nest distance — the prose and code don't fully line up

Multiclass classification on real uploaded photos is genuinely poor (18-25%); the headline 98% only holds for the collapsed true/false task on a tiny 107-photo cropped set that contains all hornet images, so it is optimistic and not cross-validated at scale
AHP judging-matrix weights are subjectively assigned by the team with no sensitivity analysis on how the final nest map shifts if the weights change
No formal uncertainty quantification or sensitivity analysis on the spatial predictions or the eradication threshold; p<0.05 cutoff is asserted by convention, not derived for this dataset
Reproduced figures/tables in the markdown are partly garbled (confusion matrix all-0.95, heatmap labels), but the underlying method descriptions are clear
Self-update rule is described qualitatively (refresh data yearly, add labeled photos) but never demonstrated or stress-tested
reusable_patterns: Decompose-and-chain architecture: turn a vague 'monitor and control X' prompt into a pipeline where each model's output is the next model's input (locate -> predict spread -> classify -> prioritize -> declare eradication), so the paper reads as one coherent system
When positive examples are scarce, reframe a binary classification into a multi-class problem then collapse it — lifts accuracy and dodges class imbalance
Cure data scarcity with NLP auto-labeling from free-text comment fields + targeted web scraping for augmentation; document counts (200/species, 13 species)
Ground every model parameter in a cited domain reference and surface the numbers (forage range, dispersal distance, active months) — turns arbitrary constants into defensible assumptions
Substitute an available proxy variable for missing data and explicitly justify the substitution (elevation for forest cover via satellite-map coincidence)
Validate a triage/ranking system by back-testing on the actual provided data and reporting before/after ranks of the known-positive items — the single most convincing evidence type for a data problem
Use AHP with a consistency check (CR<0.10) to combine heterogeneous, non-commensurable factors into transparent weights
Distance-gated weighted fusion: bucket records by spatial proximity and apply different evidence-weighting per bucket so each sub-model contributes where it is informative
Adopt an established statistic from the literature (Solow-Roberts) for the 'when can we declare victory' question rather than inventing one, then extend it with a frequency-change term
Pair a non-technical memo + a UI mockup figure with the technical body so judges see both rigor and real-world deliverability
Report failure honestly (ablation tables across model depths and data conditions, confusion-matrix error modes) — honesty reads as competence to judges
